ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for nasi lemak sambal; closest parent is chili sauce food product (FOODON_03311737).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW (Chilli sauce) is the closest defensible parent; no specific nasi lemak sambal code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for nasi lemak sambal chili sauce as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of nasi lemak sambal chili sauce is approximately 15 g (about 1 tablespoon), served as a condiment alongside nasi lemak rice, fried anchovies, peanuts, and egg.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal (chili paste condiment) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Sauces, chili sauce, hot, ready-to-serve (USDA NDB 06164) adapted with regional ingredient profile
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists sambal as a condiment category. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore data for sambal belacan / chili paste condiments and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entry for chili sauce (NDB 06164). Nasi lemak sambal is a cooked chili-shrimp paste condiment; values reflect a typical Singaporean nasi lemak sambal composition with oil, dried chili, shallots, belacan, tamarind, and sugar.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chili-based condiments and sambal — analogous estimate based on chili sauce GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nasi lemak sambal chili sauce. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogy with chili-based condiments and sauces, which are predominantly fat, protein, and fibre with moderate sugar content. The University of Sydney GI Database lists chili sauce and similar condiments in the low GI range (GI 30–45). Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (18.5 × 0.35 × 15) / 100 ≈ 1.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (620 mg per 100 g) from belacan (shrimp paste) and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit nasi lemak sambal to no more than 1 teaspoon (approximately 5 g) per serving, as even a standard 15 g tablespoon portion contributes approximately 93 mg sodium, and cumulative intake across a full nasi lemak meal can significantly exceed the recommended 2000 mg daily sodium limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (8 g per 100 g) and added sugar in traditional sambal preparation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Nasi lemak sambal has a low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) due to its fat, fibre, and protein content, but the added sugar (approximately 1.2 g per 15 g serving) and high-fat oil base warrant portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes managing postprandial glucose and caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (10 g per 100 g) with predominantly unsaturated fats, but high sodium from belacan
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat profile of nasi lemak sambal is predominantly unsaturated (monounsaturated and polyunsaturated), which is relatively favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content from shrimp paste and salt poses a risk for blood pressure elevation and should be restricted to small condiment portions in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Lemak Sambal Chili Sauce is the signature spicy condiment served as an essential component of nasi lemak, Singapore's and Malaysia's iconic national dish. The sambal is a cooked chili paste made by blending and sautéing dried chilies, fresh chilies, shallots, garlic, and belacan (fermented shrimp paste) in cooking oil, then seasoning with tamarind juice, sugar, and salt to achieve a balance of heat, sweetness, and umami. The resulting sauce is deep red-orange in colour, with a thick, slightly oily texture and an intensely aromatic, spicy-sweet-savoury flavour profile. It is served at room temperature or warm alongside coconut rice (nasi lemak), fried anchovies (ikan bilis), roasted peanuts, cucumber slices, and a hard-boiled or fried egg. Nutritionally, nasi lemak sambal provides approximately 180 kcal per 100 g, with moderate fat (10 g, predominantly unsaturated), moderate carbohydrate (18.5 g including 8 g sugar from added sugar), and modest protein (3.5 g). The sodium content is high at 620 mg per 100 g due to belacan and added salt, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), reflecting the fat, fibre, and protein matrix that slows glucose absorption. Clinically, the sambal is a high-sodium condiment that should be consumed in small quantities (1 tablespoon or less) by individuals managing blood pressure or cardiovascular risk. It is halal-certified in most commercial preparations and is gluten-free and dairy-free, though it contains crustacean allergens from belacan.

confidence_reason: HPB Singapore Foodo provides sambal as a condiment category for nasi lemak, and nutrient values are adapted from HPB data and USDA SR Legacy chili sauce entry. No single approved source provides exact per-100g values for nasi lemak sambal as a distinct food item; values represent a well-grounded adaptation from regional and international approved sources. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for chili-based condiments.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo sambal/chili condiment data and USDA SR Legacy chili sauce (NDB 06164), adjusted for the typical nasi lemak sambal ingredient profile (belacan, dried chili, shallots, oil, tamarind, sugar). Sodium is high due to belacan content. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this condiment category. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as nasi lemak sambal is prepared with vegetable cooking oil and contains no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. GI estimated at 35 (low) by analogy with chili sauce condiments per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The food_group from the input ('Oils, seasonings and condiments') is preserved as it accurately reflects the condiment nature of this item. Cuisine tag 'Toast' from the input source (Toast Box restaurant) reflects the restaurant chain context, not the cuisine of the dish itself; cuisine has been corrected to Singaporean/Malaysian.
